Every part of God’s truth and will should be presented to all who profess the gospel, and they should be urged to consider it in their hearts and consciences. We should not always speak about outward things; these have their places and use, but often take up too much attention and time, which could be better used. The humbled sinner who pleads guilty and cries for mercy cannot be discouraged by this passage, whatever his conscience may accuse him of. Nor does it prove that anyone who becomes a new creature in Christ will ever completely turn away from him. The apostle is not speaking about the falling away of mere professors who are never truly convinced or influenced by the gospel. Such people have nothing to fall away from but an empty name or a hypocritical profession. Neither is he speaking about minor declines or backslidings. Nor are those sins meant that Christians commit through strong temptations or the power of worldly or fleshly desires. But the falling away mentioned here is an open and declared rejection of Christ, stemming from hostility in heart against him, his cause, and his people, by people approving in their minds the deeds of his murderers, and all this after they have received knowledge of the truth and tasted some of its comforts. Of these it is said that it is impossible to renew them again unto repentance. Not because Christ’s blood is not sufficient to obtain forgiveness for this sin; but this sin, in its very nature, is opposed to repentance and everything that leads to it. If those who mistakenly understand this passage or their own situation fear that there is no mercy for them, they should pay attention to the description of the nature of this sin: a complete and willing rejection of Christ and his cause, and joining with his enemies. This would relieve them from unwarranted fears. We ourselves should be careful and caution others against approaching a gulf as terrible as apostasy; yet in doing so we should stay close to the word of God and be careful not to wound or terrify the weak, or discourage those who have fallen and are repentant. Believers not only taste of the word of God but they drink it in. And this fertile field or garden receives a blessing. But the merely nominal Christian, remaining unfruitful under the means of grace, or producing nothing but deceit and selfishness, was near the awful state described above; and everlasting misery was the end reserved for him. Let us watch with humble caution and prayer as to ourselves.

There are things that are never separated from salvation; things that show a person to be in a state of salvation, and which will result in eternal salvation. And the things that accompany salvation are better than anything a hypocrite or apostate has ever enjoyed. Acts of love, done for the glory of Christ, or done to his saints for Christ’s sake, as God gives opportunity, are clear signs of a person's salvation; and more sure tokens of saving grace given than the enlightenings and tastings mentioned earlier. No love is to be considered true love unless it involves action; and no works are right works which do not flow from love to Christ.

The hope here meant is a certain expectation of good things promised, through those promises, with love, desire, and valuing them. Hope has its degrees, as faith also does. The promise of blessedness God has made to believers comes from God’s eternal purpose, settled between the eternal Father, Son, and Spirit. These promises of God may safely be depended upon; for here we have two things which cannot change: God's counsel and his oath, in which it is not possible for God to lie; it would be contrary to his nature as well as to his will. And because He cannot lie, the destruction of the unbeliever and the salvation of the believer are equally certain. Observe that those to whom God has given full assurance of happiness have a right to the promises by inheritance. The consolations of God are strong enough to support his people under their heaviest trials. Here is refuge for all sinners who flee to the mercy of God, through Christ’s redemption, according to the covenant of grace, laying aside all other confidences. We are in this world as a ship at sea, tossed up and down, and in danger of being cast away. We need an anchor to keep us sure and steady. Gospel hope is our anchor in the storms of this world. It is sure and steadfast, or it could not keep us so. The free grace of God, the merits and mediation of Christ, and the powerful influences of his Spirit are the basis for this hope, and therefore it is a steadfast hope. Christ is the object and foundation of the believer’s hope. Let us therefore set our affections on things above, and wait patiently for his appearance, when we will certainly appear with him in glory.
